risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Within each pair, we will randomize practices to either: 1. the use of Depression Medication Choice within the clinical encounter; or 2. usual care. A study statistician will perform the randomization centrally after the practices have been enrolled and ensure concealment of allocation." Comment: The study explicitly states that practices were randomized centrally by a statistician, and the methods indicate paired allocation within pairs. Although the exact random number method is not named, central randomization by a statistician in this context supports a truly random allocation sequence and is not described as using any predictable rule.

Quote: "A study statistician will perform the randomization centrally after the practices have been enrolled and ensure concealment of allocation." Comment: This explicitly describes a central allocation process with concealment of the upcoming assignment, which prevents recruiters from foreseeing group assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Practices, clinicians and investigators will not be masked to allocation." Comment: This indicates that the people delivering the intervention were aware of assignment, and the intervention outcomes were largely patient- and clinician-reported or encounter-based (e.g. decisional conflict, knowledge, satisfaction, patient involvement, medication adherence), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the results across the study overall.

Quote: "Practices, clinicians and investigators will not be masked to allocation." Comment: Outcome assessment relied heavily on patient self-reported questionnaires (e.g. "Patients will complete the decisional conflict scale... immediately after the clinical encounter"; "Patients will complete the nine items of the PHQ-9..."; "Satisfaction... patients and clinicians will be asked questions") and clinician self-report, so the assessors were effectively unblinded for subjective outcomes. For the patient-reported outcomes, the patient is the assessor and was not blinded to the intervention in the encounter, making detection bias likely.

Quote: "To prevent loss to follow-up and support the intention-to-treat principle, we will centrally follow patients, and ensure that patient surveys and pharmacy follow-ups are complete." Comment: This protocol indicates active measures to minimize missing data and preserve follow-up, and it also states that all patient outcomes will be analyzed according to intention-to-treat. Although the paper does not provide observed attrition numbers (it is a protocol), the reported follow-up procedures and complete planned data collection make attrition-related bias unlikely.
